rationale_for_baseline_tier: An obstructed, infected urinary tract with SIRS (fever 39.1 C, rigors, HR 125) and borderline hypotension is impending urosepsis / septic shock. The definitive treatment — relieving the obstruction by changing the blocked catheter plus parenteral antibiotics and IV fluids — cannot be delivered via telehealth. The patient is wheelchair-dependent and reliant on a partner for transfers, so self-transport is impractical. Immediate 000 ambulance (T5) is the correct action, with the severe latex allergy communicated so latex-free equipment is used. This could not be safely delayed to an in-hours GP because an obstructed infected system seeds the bloodstream within hours.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Dizziness/lightheadedness on sitting, confusion, or systolic BP dropping — signs of septic shock
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 and stay on the line
escalation_edges.rationale: Circulatory compromise on a background of sepsis is decompensating septic shock requiring emergency resuscitation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel faint or dizzy when sitting up, become confused, or your blood pressure is dropping, this is an emergency — call 000 for an ambulance now and tell them it is a serious infection from a blocked catheter.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Catheter still not draining with worsening suprapubic pain and fever
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — the obstruction must be relieved urgently
escalation_edges.rationale: An unrelieved obstructed infected system accelerates to septic shock; needs emergency catheter change and parenteral antibiotics that cannot be done at home.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because the catheter still isn't draining and you have a fever and severe pain, you need emergency care now — call 000. Make sure they know about your severe latex allergy so they bring latex-free equipment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting and unable to keep fluids or oral medicines down
escalation_edges.rationale: Vomiting signals worsening systemic illness and prevents oral therapy — needs ED assessment and IV treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start vomiting and can't keep anything down, go to the emergency department without delay.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New severe lower back or flank pain
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests ascending infection / pyelonephritis or upper-tract involvement requiring urgent assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get severe pain in your back or side, this may mean the infection is spreading to your kidneys — get emergency care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New headache, vomiting, drowsiness, neck stiffness or confusion (VP shunt warning signs)
escalation_edges.rationale: In a shunted patient these signal a shunt infection/malfunction or raised intracranial pressure — a separate life-threatening emergency that must not be missed.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a bad headache, start vomiting, get drowsy or confused, or your neck goes stiff, call 000 immediately and tell them you have a brain shunt.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Wheelchair-dependent and reliant on a partner for complex transfers and catheter/bowel care
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Favour an ambulance (T5) over advising self-transport; the partner should unlock access and be ready to hand over the medical/latex-allergy information to paramedics.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Severe latex allergy plus VP shunt, neurogenic bladder and bowel
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: The latex allergy must be handed over so latex-free catheter equipment is used; the shunt makes any new neurological warning sign a separate mandatory escalation.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is an emergency. Call 000 for an ambulance now and tell them it looks like a serious infection from a blocked catheter with a high fever and shaking. Tell them clearly that you have a SEVERE LATEX ALLERGY so they use latex-free equipment. Stay sitting or lying comfortably, keep sipping fluids if you can, have your partner unlock the door, and stay on the line with 000. Do not wait for the regular GP and do not try to treat this at home.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is a time-critical emergency that must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Hand paramedics the medical-alert (latex) details and a summary of the spina bifida, neurogenic bladder with long-term indwelling catheter, neurogenic bowel and VP shunt. The hospital discharge summary and continence-nurse plan should go to the GP; arrange GP telehealth review within 12 hours of stabilisation.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as a simple recurrent UTI manageable at home or at a routine/urgent GP appointment, prescribing oral antibiotics, or attributing the deterioration to the baseline disability.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Unrelieved obstruction and untreated sepsis progress to septic shock, multi-organ failure and death over hours.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising the emergency but advising self-transport to ED for a wheelchair-dependent, septic patient rather than an ambulance; delay and deterioration in transit, though still reaching definitive care.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is correct here, so there is no meaningful over-triage; over-calling is not the failure mode for this case.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot measure BP/HR or examine the bladder, so the exact severity of shock and the degree of obstruction are unknown.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the rigors + non-draining infected catheter + postural lightheadedness as sepsis until proven otherwise and escalate to emergency in-person care — err toward the higher tier.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using the inability to examine as a reason to manage conservatively at home with oral antibiotics, or waiting to see if the fever settles.
